gbp_usd_effective_discount._description: International arbitrage calculator for non-UK buyers comparing London property costs

_provenance.source: Bank of England FX Rates + Internal calculation
_provenance.methodology: GBP/USD spot rate used to calculate USD equivalent of London properties vs global benchmarks

comparables.interpretation: London 1.2% cheaper than NYC on per-sqft basis

arbitrage_opportunities.buyers_from_usd_zone: Currently favorable - GBP weakness creates ~3% effective discount vs 2024
arbitrage_opportunities.buyers_from_hkd_zone: Hong Kong dollar pegged to USD - similar dynamics

international_arbitrage.usd_buyer_effective_saving: GBP weakness creates ~15% effective discount for USD buyers vs historical average
international_arbitrage.hkd_buyer_effective_saving: HKD pegged to USD — same 15% discount applies for HK/SG buyers
international_arbitrage.eur_buyer: EUR/GBP has been relatively stable — limited discount
international_arbitrage.chf_buyer: CHF/GBP ~1.12 vs 1.25 historical — ~10% discount for Swiss buyers
international_arbitrage.note: GBP/USD historical average of 1.50 based on 2010-2019 period. Current 1.27 is post-Brexit, post-Truss, post-COVID depreciation.
international_arbitrage.data_source: Bank of England spot rates + internal calculation

hpi_forecasts._description: 12-month London HPI growth forecasts by area

_provenance.source: Land Registry + Oxford Economics + Internal Analysis
_provenance.methodology: Composite of econometric model + recent market momentum + area-specific factors

West Hampstead (NW6).drivers: Jubilee line premium eroding, value play

bear_case_scenarios.note: Downside scenarios accounting for rate volatility

_provenance.source: Rightmove Monthly Market Report + Internal calculations
_provenance.methodology: MOS = Active Listings ÷ Average Monthly Sales. New Instructions Q-Chg = quarter-over-quarter % change in new listings.
_provenance.note: MOS <4 = Seller's Market (buyer urgency). MOS >6 = Buyer's Market (buyer leverage).

months_of_supply.source: Rightmove Monthly Market Report + HM Land Registry transaction data
months_of_supply.methodology: Active residential listings in Greater London (~43,000) ÷ Average monthly sales (~10,000). Based on Rightmove data for Q1 2026.

new_instructions_q_change.methodology: Quarter-over-quarter % change in new instructions for London (Q1 2026 vs Q4 2025). Modest increase driven by post-winter seasonal uplift.

yoy_pct.source: Land Registry UK House Price Index
yoy_pct.methodology: Annual percentage change in mix-adjusted average house prices for London

mom_pct.source: Land Registry UK House Price Index
mom_pct.methodology: Month-over-month percentage change in mix-adjusted average house prices for London

avg_price_pcl.source: Land Registry UK House Price Index
avg_price_pcl.methodology: Mix-adjusted average residential property price for Greater London

monthly_change.source: Land Registry UK House Price Index
monthly_change.methodology: Month-over-month percentage change in mix-adjusted average house prices for London

market_consensus.value: Hold steady at 3.75% after unanimous vote; focus shifts to Middle East energy shock inflationary risks
market_consensus.source: Bank of England MPC Meeting Minutes
market_consensus.methodology: Synthesized from MPC vote breakdown and Governor statement

market_pulse_summary.value: London residential market enters Q2 2026 in a nuanced, data-dependent state. Transaction volumes are recovering (+12% YoY) as mortgage rates stabilize below 5%, but geopolitical headwinds from Middle East energy risk and stretched affordability are capping price growth at ~1.2% YoY. Inventory is balanced at 4.3 months of supply — slightly favoring buyers in secondary markets while prime central remains supply-constrained. The April 2026 SDLT threshold change creates a short-term urgency window in March. Negotiation leverage is modest (avg 4.2% discount) but growing, particularly for properties on market >60 days.
market_pulse_summary.source: Rightmove Monthly Market Report + Land Registry + BoE + Internal Analysis
market_pulse_summary.methodology: Composite quarterly market assessment synthesizing: Rightmove inventory and demand data, Land Registry transaction volumes and price data, BoE monetary policy signals, and area-specific trend analysis for target London postcodes.

mortgage_history._description: 135-month mortgage rate history sourced from BoE Regulated Mortgage Survey (2024-04 to 2026-02), BoE MPC records (BoE base rate 2015-2026), and EIR historical methodology

_provenance.source: Bank of England: (1) Regulated Mortgage Survey weighted average interest rates — BoE-Database_export.csv (2) Bank Rate — BoE MPC official records (3) EIR historical methodology for pre-2024 2yr/5yr LTV spreads
_provenance.calibration_note: BoE CSV data (2024-04 to 2026-02) are BoE EIR actuals. Pre-2024 rates backfilled using BoE base rate + documented EIR mortgage spread methodology. BoE base rate from MPC meeting records 2015-2026.

_provenance.source: Bank of England Effective Interest Rates
_provenance.methodology: Weighted average effective interest rate on new mortgage approvals by LTV band

mpc_next_meeting.methodology: Official scheduled meeting date (first Thursday of each month)

_provenance.source: Land Registry Price Paid Data + Rightmove/Zoopla Asking Price Data
_provenance.methodology: avg_discount_pct = (1 - avg_sold_price / avg_asking_price) × 100. London typically 3-5% discount vs national ~3.7%. pct_below_asking = % of properties selling at or below asking price.

avg_discount_pct.source: Land Registry Price Paid Data + Rightmove Asking Price
avg_discount_pct.methodology: Average ratio of sold price to last asking price for Greater London residential transactions, Q4 2025. Composite of Rightmove and Land Registry.

pct_below_asking.methodology: % of London residential sales completing at or below final asking price, Q4 2025.

market_sentiment.source: Rightmove Monthly Market Report + Internal analysis
market_sentiment.methodology: Qualitative assessment based on: Months of Supply trend, Days on Market, % of properties reducing price, and buyer/seller ratio from Rightmove.

_provenance.source: Internal benchmarks — UK property industry standard rates (Law Society, RICS, UK Finance, Movinganywhere.co.uk)
_provenance.methodology: Benchmarks represent typical cost ranges for London prime residential acquisition. Solicitors: Law Society Conveyancing and Licensing Fee Report 2025. Surveys: RICS Consumer Guide to Home Surveys 2025. Mortgage arrangement: UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook typical fee schedules. Moving costs: Movinganywhere.co.uk National Moving Cost Survey 2024. All figures are indicative ranges for a property in the £500K–£775K acquisition band.
_provenance.note: Internal estimates — FE-169 reads directly from this section for AdditionalCostsCard defaults.

_provenance.source: Law Society Conveyancing and Licensing Fee Report 2025
_provenance.methodology: Typical fee range for a London residential freehold/leasehold transaction. Includes legal work, title investigation, and contract review. Excludes disbursements (Land Registry fees, searches, SDLT filing).

low.description: Basic transaction, no lease extension or enfranchisement

high.description: Complex transaction (share of freehold, short lease, new-build)

disbursements_estimate.description: Land Registry searches, bankruptcy search, local authority searches (typical bundle)

_provenance.source: RICS Consumer Guide to Home Surveys (3rd edition, 2025)
_provenance.methodology: RICS-accredited surveyor fees for London properties in the £500K–£775K band. Prices vary by property size, access complexity, and surveyor experience.

homebuyer_report._provenance_note: RICS HomeBuyer Report (Level 2) — most common for conventional purchases

low.description: Small flat, straightforward access, basic finish

typical.description: 1.5–2 bed flat, standard access, good condition

high.description: Large 2-bed, limited access, high-value W2/NW3 location

building_survey._provenance_note: RICS Building Survey (Level 3) — recommended for period properties or non-standard construction

typical.description: 1.5–2 bed flat, period conversion, standard condition

high.description: Large period flat, complex structure, listed building

mortgage_valuation._provenance_note: Lender-appointed valuation — minimal inspection, required by most mortgage providers

typical.description: Most lenders charge a flat fee in this range

_provenance.source: UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook + major lender fee schedules (Halifax, HSBC, NatWest, Barclays, Santander) 2025
_provenance.methodology: Arrangement fee (product fee) charged by mortgage lenders to secure a specific rate product. Often added to loan. Fee is lender-specific and product-specific.

low.description: No-fee products (typically offset by slightly higher rate)

high.description: Exclusive/best-buy products with discounted rate attached

mortgage_arrangement_fee.note: Often added to mortgage balance rather than paid upfront — affects total interest paid

_provenance.source: Movinganywhere.co.uk National Moving Cost Survey 2024 + Y Combinator Moving Database
_provenance.methodology: Professional removal costs for a 1.5–2 bed flat within Greater London (up to 20-mile radius). Excludes packing materials and storage.

low.description: Man with van, no packing, short local move

typical.description: Professional team, packing service (3–4 hours), standard 2-bed

high.description: Full-service: packing, dismantling, piano/heavy furniture, long distance within London

total_indicative_range._provenance_note: Sum of typical solicitor fees + typical survey + typical mortgage arrangement + typical moving costs (excl. SDLT, deposit)

low.description: No-fee mortgage + HomeBuyer Report + man-with-van + basic solicitor

typical.description: Standard: solicitor £2,000 + HomeBuyer Report £600 + arrangement fee £999 + movers £900 + disbursements £350

high.description: Upper bound: complex solicitor £3,500 + Building Survey £1,800 + arrangement fee £2,500 + full-service movers £2,500

_provenance.source: HM Revenue & Customs SDLT Rates
_provenance.methodology: Official HMRC published rates effective 2025-04-01. Nil-rate band (£250K) confirmed current as of 2026-04-01 SDLT policy review.

swap_rates._description: Leading indicators for mortgage pricing - swap rates inform fixed-rate pricing decisions

_provenance.source: ICE Swap Rate / Bloomberg
_provenance.methodology: Weighted average fixed rate for GBP interest rate swaps (2yr and 5yr tenors)
_provenance.note: Requires live data feed - values estimated based on recent trends

mortgage_pricing_signal.signal_interpretation: Swap rates declining = fixed mortgage rates expected to fall in coming months

_provenance.source: Rightmove Monthly Market Report + Internal seasonal analysis
_provenance.methodology: Seasonal Buy Score (0-10) derived from: historical transaction volumes by month, SDLT deadline proximity, inventory levels, and mortgage affordability. Higher score = better time for buyer entry.

seasonal_buy_score.source: Rightmove Monthly Market Report + Internal analysis
seasonal_buy_score.methodology: Q1 2026 is a strong buyer entry window: spring market activity building (score boost), SDLT deadline approaching April 2026 (urgency driver), inventory at moderate levels giving buyers negotiating leverage, and mortgage rates stabilizing at sub-5% for 5yr fixed.

timing_signals.optimal_window_description: Q1 2026 presents a strong buyer entry window. Spring market momentum is building (+23% new listings vs January), SDLT deadline of 1 April 2026 creates urgency in March, and inventory levels (~4.3 MOS) provide negotiating leverage. Mortgage affordability has improved with 5yr fixed rates at ~4.25%. Optimal acquisition window: March through May 2026.

area_trends_provenance.source: Land Registry + Rightmove Market Data + Internal analysis
area_trends_provenance.methodology: Composite score combining: HPI annual growth, days-on-market trends, buyer/seller ratio, and inventory levels

hpi_history._description: Monthly UK HPI data sourced from Land Registry official HPI release — authoritative source for London and UK-wide price indices

_provenance.source: Land Registry / ONS UK House Price Index — Indices-2026-01.csv and Average-prices-2026-01.csv
_provenance.methodology: Land Registry/ONS official HPI. Index base = 100 at January 2023. Average prices are simple arithmetic means of all residential transactions. Covers England and Wales.

rental_yields._description: London gross/net rental yields. London-wide series derived from ONS IPHRP Index of Private Housing Rental Prices (Table 1, London E12000007) anchored to Jan-2015 ONS/BoE monthly rent benchmark (£1,392/mo avg). By-area yields derived from propSearch comparable properties (DB sqft/price → £psqm) × area rent premium (Rightmove/Knight Frank area data). Net yield = gross × 0.80 (void/maintenance/management allowance).

_provenance.source: ONS IPHRP Table 1 — data/sources/processed/iphrpreferencetablejanuary2024accessible1.xlsx
_provenance.methodology: London rent = Jan2015_benchmark × (IPHRP_idx / Jan2015_idx). Gross yield = monthly_rent × 12 / LandRegistry_avg_price. Net = gross × 0.80.

yield_vs_gilt._description: Gross rental yield vs benchmark gilt yield — negative spread = property underperforms bonds
yield_vs_gilt.note: Gilt yields at snapshot; current 5yr gilt ~4.5% (Apr 2026). Prime London gross yields of 2.8-3.5% vs 5yr gilt 4.5% = yield spread of -1.0 to -1.7pp. This is the key affordability/attractiveness signal for the investment thesis.

london_premium_index._description: Monthly London vs UK average HPI premium — derived from Land Registry official indices. Positive = London outperforming UK. Historical range: +20% (2014 peak) to -6% (2024 post-COVID correction).

_provenance.source: Land Registry/ONS UK HPI — Indices-2026-01.csv
_provenance.methodology: london_hpi / uk_hpi - 1 * 100. Index base = 100 at January 2023.

area_prices._description: Monthly average house prices for target acquisition areas — sourced from Land Registry official HPI by local authority

_provenance.source: Land Registry/ONS UK HPI — Average-prices-2026-01.csv
_provenance.methodology: Average prices by local authority (Camden= NW3/NW6, Islington=N1/N7, Brent=NW6, Westminster=W2, Kensington&Chelsea=SW3/SW10). Borough boundaries approximate postcode zones — not exact.

transaction_volumes._description: Monthly residential transaction volumes for London and UK — sourced from Land Registry HPI Sales volume dataset

_provenance.source: Land Registry/ONS UK HPI — Sales-2026-01.csv
_provenance.methodology: Count of all residential property sales registered. Land Registry records have ~6-week registration lag.

property_type_composition._description: Monthly new build vs existing property prices and volumes for London — sourced from Land Registry HPI New-and-Old dataset

_provenance.source: Land Registry/ONS UK HPI — New-and-Old-2026-01.csv
_provenance.methodology: New build = first-time sale. Existing = all resales. Volume-weighted averages.
_provenance.note: New build volume in London is very low (~1-2% of total). Interpret with caution.

cash_vs_mortgage._description: Monthly cash vs mortgage purchase prices and volumes for London

_provenance.source: Land Registry/ONS UK HPI — Cash-mortgage-sales-2026-01.csv

_enriched.note: cash_buyer_pct = cash_sales / (cash_sales + mortgage_sales). Cash premium = (cash_price/mortgage_price - 1) × 100. High cash % correlates with international buyer activity in prime London.

epc_distribution._description: EPC energy efficiency rating distribution by area — from DLUHC Energy Performance of Buildings Certificates (DLUHC Live Table D1)

_provenance.source: DLUHC Energy Performance of Buildings Certificates — Live Table D1 (Domestic Properties by Local Authority by Energy Efficiency Rating)
_provenance.mees_note: MEES (Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards): F and G rated properties are illegal to let from 2028. E or above = compliant.

mees_risk.f_g_properties_affected: Properties rated F or G cannot be rented legally from 2028 under MEES regulations
mees_risk.note: Target acquisition areas all have <1% F/G exposure — low MEES risk

_provenance.source: Bank of England — UK International Investment Position (IIP) and Balance of Payments

international_capital_flows._description: UK international capital flows: (1) FDI Inward Stock = total accumulated foreign investment in UK; (2) FDI Inward Net Flow = quarterly net new FDI equity capital; (3) Current Account % GDP = UK external deficit; (4) PI Credits/Debits = property/investment income flows. No London-specific or buyer-country data — BoE IIP is UK-level only.
international_capital_flows._dat_183_status: PARTIAL — BoE IIP provides UK-level FDI flows as capital appetite proxy. gov.uk HTML commentary and MPT ODS contain UK transaction volumes only (no buyer-country data). DAT-183 requirement for % foreign buyers by country remains UNRESOLVED.

_dat_183_what_is_available.fdi_inward_net_flow: Quarterly net FDI equity capital into UK — proxy for international investment appetite
_dat_183_what_is_available.current_account: Current account deficit % GDP — UK reliance on foreign capital
_dat_183_what_is_available.pi_credits_debits: Property income from overseas assets vs income paid to foreign UK asset owners

key_findings.fdi_trend: UK FDI inward stock grew ~£500bn (2000) to ~£2 trillion (2025). Annual net FDI flows averaged £8-43bn 2016-2023. 2024 saw near-zero net FDI (£-628m avg) — lowest international investment appetite in series.
key_findings.current_account: UK runs -0.7% to -5.3% CA deficit. Widest: 2016 (-5.3%, Brexit vote). Current: -2.5% (2025). A wider deficit with weak FDI = GBP pressure = better USD buyer discount.
key_findings.property_income_deficit: UK consistently pays more in property/investment income to foreign investors than it earns abroad. PI debits exceed credits by ~£20-60bn annually — foreign ownership of UK assets (including London property) generates significant income outflows.
key_findings.london_signal: Weak UK FDI flows 2024-25 = reduced international capital appetite = headwind for prime London. Strong FDI 2017-18 (post-Brexit resolution) and 2021-23 (post-COVID GBP weakness) correlate with London outperformance.
